back: Trustee companies: responsible for total assets of not less than HK$40 million. Corporations or partnerships: either a portfolio of not less than HK$8 million OR total assets of not less than HK$40 million (or foreign currency equivalents).
explanation: The corporation/partnership tests are alternatives – meeting either one suffices. Wholly owned investment holding companies of qualifying persons and corporations which wholly own a qualifying corporation can also be Corporate PIs. (PDF p.207)

front: When must a licensed or registered person report matters listed in paragraph 12.5 of the Code of Conduct (e.g. material breaches, insolvency situations, disciplinary actions) to the SFC?
back: Immediately upon discovery.
explanation: The reportable matters include material or suspected material breaches of laws, rules and codes; insolvency situations affecting the firm, its substantial shareholders or directors; disciplinary actions by regulators; material problems with business systems or equipment; suspected material breaches of SFO market misconduct provisions by clients; and any determination or settlement of a complaint with the FDRS. (PDF p.203)

section: General Principle 9 – responsibility of senior management
front: List the eight managers-in-charge (MIC) core functions identified by the SFC for licensed corporations.
back: (1) Overall management oversight; (2) key business line; (3) operational control and review; (4) risk management; (5) finance and accounting; (6) information technology; (7) compliance; (8) anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing.
explanation: Senior management of a licensed corporation comprises all directors (including shadow directors), responsible officers, and each individual principally responsible for one or more of the eight MIC core functions. An MIC need not be a licensed representative, but changes to an MIC's appointment must be notified to the SFC as for licensed staff. (PDF p.205)

section: Provisions of the SFO not applicable to PIs
front: From which SFO provisions are ALL categories of professional investor excluded?
back: s. 174, SFO (unsolicited calls – PIs may be cold-called) and s. 175, SFO (requirements for offers by intermediaries or representatives).
explanation: These exclusions apply to Institutional, Corporate and Individual PIs alike. The PI definition may also be relevant for certain licensing and product authorisation purposes. (PDF p.208)

front: After how long a period of no investment activity in a particular product or market must an additional PI assessment of a Corporate PI be undertaken?
back: More than two years.
explanation: Separate assessments should be made for different product types or markets where relevant. All assessments made for applying PI exemptions must be recorded in writing and the relevant documentation kept. (PDF p.210)

section: Conduct requirements in relation to over-the-counter derivative ("OTCD") transactions
front: What are the two new regulated activities introduced in the SFO for OTC derivatives, and when will they take effect?
back: Type 11: dealing in OTC derivative products or advising on OTC derivative products. Type 12: providing client clearing services for OTC derivative transactions. They will be implemented when the relevant commencement notice is issued.
explanation: These activities form part of the post-2008 reform that introduced Part IIIA of the SFO, supported by detailed subsidiary legislation, to reduce systemic risks in the OTCD market through reporting, trading and clearing requirements. (PDF p.211)

section: Mitigating risk in OTCD transactions that are not centrally cleared
front: What are the four key areas of the SFC's risk mitigation requirements for non-centrally cleared OTCD transactions, and where are they set out?
back: Documentation, valuation, portfolio matters and handling of disputes – set out in Part I, Schedule 10 of the Code of Conduct.
explanation: The requirements apply only to OTCD transactions that are not centrally cleared, and they apply to licensed corporations (a contracting counterparty, or a Type 9 asset manager managing a portfolio of NCC OTCDs for a CIS); they do not apply to registered persons. (PDF p.212)

section: Margin requirements for non-centrally cleared OTCD transactions
front: Where VM needs to be exchanged for NCC OTCD transactions, how often must it be calculated and when must it be collected?
back: VM must be calculated at least on a daily basis and collected as soon as practicable.
explanation: This applies where the average aggregate notional amount of NCC OTCDs exceeds the relevant thresholds so that IM and VM must generally be exchanged. The daily-calculation rule applies specifically to VM, which tracks current mark-to-market exposure. (PDF p.215)

section: Margin requirements for non-centrally cleared OTCD transactions
front: Which parties are specifically excluded from the definition of "covered entity" under the NCC OTCD margin requirements?
back: Sovereigns, public sector entities, multilateral development banks as specified by the HKMA, and the Bank for International Settlements.
explanation: "Covered entity" otherwise includes both financial and non-financial counterparties that, together with their group companies, have an average aggregate notional amount of NCC OTCD exceeding the relevant thresholds. These named public-sector-type bodies are carved out. (PDF p.214)

section: Margin requirements for non-centrally cleared OTCD transactions
front: Who regulates registered institutions in respect of IM and VM requirements for NCC OTCD transactions?
back: The HKMA — the Code of Conduct IM/VM requirements do not apply to registered institutions.
explanation: The Code of Conduct margin requirements apply to licensed persons contracting with covered entities, subject to de minimis thresholds. A registered institution may nevertheless be regarded as a financial counterparty when a licensed person determines its own IM/VM obligations. (PDF p.214)

section: Schedules specifying additional conduct requirements for dealings on The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited and the Hong Kong Futures Exchange Limited, in securities margin financing, leveraged foreign exchange trading, electronic trading and ALPs
front: Which regulated activity types are covered by Schedule 5 (margin lending) and Schedule 6 (leveraged foreign exchange trading) of the Code of Conduct?
back: Schedule 5: securities margin financiers (Type 8) and securities dealers conducting margin financing (Type 1), plus their representatives. Schedule 6: leveraged foreign exchange traders (Type 3).
explanation: These schedules specify conduct and operational requirements for the named activities. By contrast, Schedule 7 covers electronic trading, Schedule 8 covers alternative liquidity pools, and Schedule 11 covers Type 13 regulated activity. (PDF p.217)

section: Schedules specifying additional conduct requirements for dealings on The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited and the Hong Kong Futures Exchange Limited, in securities margin financing, leveraged foreign exchange trading, electronic trading and ALPs
front: Schedule 11 of the Code of Conduct sets out additional requirements for which regulated activity?
back: Type 13 regulated activity — providing depositary services for relevant CISs.
explanation: Schedule 11 applies to persons licensed or registered for Type 13 regulated activity. It sits alongside Schedule 7 (electronic trading) and Schedule 8 (alternative liquidity pools) among the conduct schedules. (PDF p.218)

front: Under the FMCC, what are the time limits for personal account dealing permission and the minimum holding period for personal investments by relevant persons?
back: Prior written permission (from the compliance officer or other designated person) is valid for no more than five trading days; personal investments must be held for at least 30 days unless prior written approval is obtained.
explanation: These FMCC guidelines for relevant persons closely reflect those in the Code of Conduct and must be included in the Fund Manager's internal rules. As a minimum the guidelines cover trading in securities and derivatives, but may extend to other investments. (PDF p.224)

front: How often should senior management ensure the Fund Manager's performance in managing funds is reviewed?
back: At least annually, whether the funds are authorised or unauthorised.
explanation: Senior management responsibilities under the FMCC are generally similar to those in the Code of Conduct, with this annual performance review obligation as an addition. The review covers the Fund Manager's performance in managing funds whether authorised or unauthorised. (PDF p.225)

front: Under the FMCC, which type of cross trade is prohibited outright, and what consent is needed for cross trades between the house account and client accounts?
back: Cross trades between staff personal accounts and client accounts are prohibited. House account–client account cross trades require the prior written consent of the client, with disclosure of any actual or potential conflicts of interest.
explanation: Cross trades between client accounts are permitted if conducted fairly so that no client is disadvantaged, and the client must be informed of them. "Client" here also includes investors of discretionary accounts managed by the same Fund Manager. (PDF p.222)

front: How often must a CRA review a rating target's creditworthiness, in any event?
back: At least annually.
explanation: A CRA should monitor issued ratings on an ongoing basis and update them as necessary (except where a rating clearly indicates it does not entail ongoing surveillance), but in any event must review the rating target's creditworthiness at least annually. (PDF p.230)

front: How often must a CRA's methodologies and models be formally reviewed, and what must happen to the findings?
back: A rigorous and formal review at least annually; findings must be recorded in writing and copied to the SFC upon completion.
explanation: This applies to the CRA's methodologies and models and any significant changes made to them. The written findings must be copied to the SFC when the review is completed. (PDF p.231)

front: Under the CRA Code, who counts as an "immediate relation" of a CRA representative?
back: A spouse, partner, parent, child, or sibling.
explanation: A representative whose immediate relation currently works for a rated entity should not be involved in or in any way influence the rating of that rating target. (PDF p.231)

front: Under the CFA Code, who monitors personal account dealings, and what system must be maintained?
back: All personal account dealings are monitored by the designated compliance officer; a watch list and restricted list system must be maintained for monitoring personal account dealings and proprietary trading.
explanation: The CFA Code's personal account dealing provisions apply to "relevant persons" — employees or directors likely to have access to confidential information on a matter where the adviser is advising on corporate finance. (PDF p.229)

front: Who are the key operators of an OFC?
back: Its director(s), investment managers and custodian.
explanation: The OFC Code applies to both public and private OFCs and their key operators. Public OFCs and their key operators must additionally comply with the SFC Handbook for Unit Trusts and Mutual Funds, Investment-Linked Assurance Schemes and Unlisted Structured Investment Products. (PDF p.234)

front: For how long must an OFC's investment manager retain its trading, accounting and other records, and where?
back: For a period of not less than seven years, in premises approved by the SFC.
explanation: The records must be sufficient to explain and reflect the financial position and operation of the investment manager's activities, and sufficient to enable an audit to be properly carried out. (PDF p.236)

front: Within what period must an OFC's audited accounts be disclosed?
back: Annually, within four months of the OFC's financial year end.
explanation: The auditor must be independent of the investment manager, the custodian and the directors of the OFC, and accounts must be prepared on the basis of Hong Kong Financial Reporting Standards or International Financial Reporting Standards (or another basis approved by the SFC case by case). (PDF p.237)

section: The CRA's own code of conduct
front: What must a CRA do with its own code of conduct ("House Code"), and who must be identified?
back: The House Code must fully implement the specific provisions of the CRA Code, be disclosed to the public on the CRA's webpage, and indicate how it will be enforced; one person must be identified as a compliance officer responsible for compliance matters.
explanation: The compliance officer is responsible for ensuring the CRA and its employees comply with both the House Code and any applicable law, rules, regulations, codes and other requirements. (PDF p.233)

front: Within what time limit must an OFC file changes to its offering document with the SFC?
back: Within seven days of issuing the revised offering document. (The original offering document must be filed with the SFC as soon as practicable following its issuance.)
explanation: An OFC files its offering document with the SFC as soon as practicable after issuance, but any subsequent changes are subject to a hard deadline of seven days from issuing the revised document. (PDF p.238)

front: For how long must a custodian of a private OFC keep accounting and other records, and where must they be kept?
back: Not less than seven years, kept in the designated premises approved by the SFC. The records must provide an audit trail for all scheme property under custody and demonstrate compliance with all applicable regulatory requirements.
explanation: The seven-year minimum retention period and the requirement that records be kept at SFC-approved designated premises are hard requirements on private OFC custodians, supporting an audit trail for all scheme property that is or has been under custody. (PDF p.239)

section: The Code of Conduct for Share Registrars
front: What must a share registrar do to be 'approved' to maintain a register of shareholders or warrant-holders of a listed company in Hong Kong?
back: It must be a member of the Federation of Share Registrars Limited. The Listing Rules state that only an approved share registrar may be employed for this purpose, and approved registrars are subject to The Code of Conduct for Share Registrars issued by the SFC in consultation with the Federation.
explanation: Membership of the Federation of Share Registrars Limited is the approval condition; the Listing Rules then restrict listed companies to using approved registrars, who are bound by the SFC-issued code. (PDF p.241)

front: What is the difference between a public OFC and a private OFC?
back: A public OFC is one that has applied for, or will apply for, authorisation under s. 104, SFO; a private OFC will not make any application under that section.
explanation: The distinction turns entirely on s. 104, SFO authorisation: public OFCs seek (or will seek) it, while private OFCs do not apply under that section at all. (PDF p.243)

front: What are the eight key areas of internal control and management systems identified in the ICG?
back: Management and supervision; segregation of duties and functions; personnel and training; information management; compliance; audit; operational controls; and risk management.
explanation: The ICG identify these eight key areas (collectively "internal controls") to match the nine general principles of conduct in the Code of Conduct. Each key area is presented as an initial objective supplemented by control guidelines on how it may be achieved. (PDF p.248)

front: Do the ICG have the force of law, and what happens if a licensed person fails to observe them?
back: No force of law — a failure to observe them is not an offence leading to prosecution, but a failure to follow their substance may reflect adversely on the person's fitness and properness to continue to be licensed or registered.
explanation: The ICG are like the codes in this respect. The practical sanction is regulatory, going to fitness and properness, rather than criminal prosecution. (PDF p.248)

section: Internal control and internal management systems
front: Internal controls give a licensed or registered person reasonable assurance that it can do which four things?
back: (a) Operate its business in an orderly and efficient manner; (b) safeguard the assets of its clients and its own; (c) maintain proper records and reliable financial and other information; and (d) comply with all applicable laws and regulatory requirements.
explanation: Internal controls are the entire system of policies, procedures, checks, controls and division of responsibilities installed to run the business. These four assurances are the stated purposes of that system. (PDF p.249)

front: When must a licensed corporation obtain prior SFC approval in connection with an external electronic data storage provider ("EDSP")?
back: Where the licensed corporation intends not to store its records as required by relevant laws and regulations but exclusively with an EDSP. Using an EDSP never relieves the firm of its record keeping obligations under the SFO or AMLO.
explanation: Firms using EDSPs must also conduct proper due diligence on the EDSP's controls, ensure it maintains a proper information security policy, ensure well-defined responsibilities between the parties, and be aware of concentration risk where the EDSP serves a large number of financial firms. (PDF p.252)

front: For anti-bribery policies, which statutory definition of "advantages" must an intermediary adopt?
back: The statutory definition of "advantages" as stated in s. 2(1) of the Prevention of Bribery Ordinance.
explanation: The compliance objective requires the intermediary to establish anti-bribery policies and codes of conduct with essential probity requirements for its directors, staff members and agents, adopting this statutory definition. (PDF p.252)

front: Which paragraphs and schedules of the Code of Conduct contain the SFC's requirements for electronic trading and for ALPs?
back: Electronic trading: Paragraph 18 and Schedule 7. Alternative liquidity pools ("ALPs"): Paragraph 19 and Schedule 8.
explanation: These are the Code of Conduct provisions stipulating the SFC's regulatory requirements for the two regimes. Confusing the paragraph/schedule pairing between the two regimes is a common error. (PDF p.246)

front: How must the compliance function be positioned within a licensed or registered firm under the ICG?
back: It must be independent of all operational and business functions and report directly to senior management; compliance staff must have the necessary skills, qualifications and experience, and be given full access, as far as practicable, to all necessary records, documentation and all areas of the business.
explanation: Independence and a direct reporting line to senior management are the key structural requirements. Full access to records and the business enables comprehensive compliance procedures. (PDF p.252)

front: To whom must material non-compliance with legal and regulatory requirements by the intermediary or its staff be reported, and how quickly?
back: Promptly to the appropriate regulators. In addition, compliance staff must promptly report material breaches of legal and regulatory requirements and of the firm's own policies and procedures to senior management.
explanation: There are two reporting limbs: internal prompt reporting by compliance staff to senior management for material breaches, and external prompt reporting of material non-compliance with legal and regulatory requirements to the appropriate regulators. (PDF p.253)

front: What client information must be obtained and confirmed before opening an account?
back: The true identity of every client, the beneficial owner of each client account, the persons authorised to give instructions for its operation, and information regarding the client's financial position, investment experience and objectives.
explanation: This is the first operational controls guideline for senior management. The information regarding the client's financial position, investment experience and objectives is expressly to be obtained before opening the account. (PDF p.255)

front: What does a complete audit trail for client orders require under the ICG operational controls?
back: Records and times of orders received from clients or generated internally, from origination through execution and settlement, using sequential numbering and time-stamping; client orders must also be handled fairly and allocated in a fair and timely manner.
explanation: Sequential numbering and time-stamping across the full life of the order (origination, execution, settlement) are the precise mechanics specified. Fair handling and fair, timely allocation of client orders are stated alongside the audit trail requirement. (PDF p.255)

front: What is the maximum penalty for a breach of the AMLO, and how does it change where there is intent to defraud?
back: Standard maximum: a fine of HK$1 million and imprisonment for two years. With intent to defraud a relevant authority (e.g. HKMA or SFC) or the firm: a fine of HK$1 million and a maximum of seven years' imprisonment.
explanation: A breach of the AMLO is a criminal offence, and employees or managers who knowingly cause or permit the firm to breach a specified provision also commit a criminal offence. The intent-to-defraud variant raises only the imprisonment term to seven years; the fine stays at HK$1 million. A breach also attracts regulatory discipline. (PDF p.261)

front: What is the maximum penalty under the WMD(CPS)O for an intermediary that continues providing services it has reasonable grounds to suspect are connected with WMD proliferation?
back: A fine of an unlimited amount and/or imprisonment of up to seven years.
explanation: The WMD(CPS)O prohibits providing any services that may be connected to the proliferation of weapons capable of causing mass destruction, including their development, production, acquisition or stockpiling, and "services" is widely defined to include financial assistance. The offence applies irrespective of where the weapons are developed. Note the contrast with the AMLO, where the fine is capped at HK$1 million. (PDF p.263)

front: Under the AMLO, who is the "relevant authority" for (a) licensed corporations and licensed VAS providers, and (b) authorised institutions?
back: (a) The SFC; (b) the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA).
explanation: The AMLO empowers certain bodies as a "relevant authority" with various powers, including investigation and prosecution, and the power to supervise and discipline financial institutions for non-compliance. Matching the right regulator to the right type of firm is a common exam point: SFC for licensed corporations and licensed VAS providers, HKMA for authorised institutions. (PDF p.261)

front: Who must approve the institutional risk assessment of a licensed corporation or licensed VAS provider, and how often must it be updated?
back: It should be approved by senior management and updated at least every two years.
explanation: The risk-based approach requires two documented risk assessments: an institutional risk assessment supporting the adoption of appropriate AML/CFT Systems in view of the nature, size and complexity of the business, and a customer risk assessment that varies with the assessed risk of each customer or business relationship. The two-year minimum update cycle applies to the institutional assessment. (PDF p.265)

front: What are the four main categories of risk factors normally taken holistically into account in ML/TF risk assessments?
back: Country risk, customer risk, product/service/transaction risk, and delivery/distribution channel risk.
explanation: There is no definitive set of risk factors that must be considered, as it depends on the circumstances of the firm and its customers, but these four categories are normally taken into account holistically. Appendix A to the GAML provides examples of what might indicate higher or lower risks. (PDF p.266)

section: Regulation by the Securities and Futures Commission
front: Name and define the three common stages of money laundering described in the GAML.
back: 1. Placement: disposal of cash proceeds from illegal activities into the financial system. 2. Layering: separating illicit proceeds from their source through layers of financial transactions to hide the source, subvert the audit trail and provide anonymity. 3. Integration: creating an impression of apparent legitimacy so the laundered proceeds re-enter the general financial system unconnected with their illegal source.
explanation: Layering is the stage at which licensed corporations and licensed VAS providers are most likely to become involved, because the liquidity of the securities, futures and virtual asset markets makes complicated trails possible. Placement is harder in these markets because SFC-regulated activities are generally not cash-based. (PDF p.264)

front: Which bodies set up and operate the Joint Financial Intelligence Unit (JFIU)?
back: The Hong Kong Police Force and the Customs and Excise Department.
explanation: Under the DTRPO, a person who knows or suspects property relates to drug trafficking should report as soon as reasonable to an authorised officer, who may be a police officer, a customs and excise officer or the JFIU; a report may also be made to a person designated by the employer. The JFIU is also the MLRO's main point of contact along with law enforcement agencies. (PDF p.262)

front: Where in the AMLO are the customer due diligence (CDD) and record-keeping requirements set out?
back: In Schedule 2 to the AMLO.
explanation: The AMLO is primarily concerned with imposing CDD requirements, imposing record-keeping requirements, giving relevant authorities powers to investigate, supervise and discipline financial institutions, and establishing a disciplinary review tribunal. The detailed CDD and record-keeping rules sit in Schedule 2. (PDF p.261)

front: Under the ICG risk management control guidelines, when must trading and position limits for proprietary trading be monitored, and what review applies to the limits?
back: They must be monitored at the end of the trading day, and the limits must be reviewed on a timely basis for effectiveness.
explanation: The control guidelines also call for a risk management function of suitably qualified and experienced professionals, procedures limiting exposure to loss from client default or changing market conditions, comprehensive risk-focused reviews at suitable intervals and whenever there are significant changes in business, operations or key personnel, regular reporting of exposures to senior management, and a senior-management-defined risk policy. (PDF p.257)

section: Regulation by the Securities and Futures Commission
front: How does the SFC's MIC regime address AML/CFT responsibility?
back: AML/CFT is one of the eight core functions of senior management, and at least one person must be appointed as the Manager-In-Charge (MIC) responsible for that function.
explanation: The SFC expects licensed corporations and licensed VAS providers to implement AML/CFT measures that are effective in detecting and preventing ML/TF, and the MIC regime reflects this expectation at senior management level. Remember the number eight and the minimum of one appointed MIC for the function. (PDF p.263)

front: How long must CDD documentation relating to customers be kept under the GAML?
back: Throughout the continuance of the business relationship and for at least five years after the end of the relationship.
explanation: The GAML record-keeping requirements support law enforcement investigations of ML/TF matters. The five-year clock for customer documents starts when the business relationship ends, which is different from transaction records, whose five years run from completion of the transaction. (PDF p.268)

front: How long must records relating to transactions be kept under the GAML?
back: At least five years following completion of the transaction, irrespective of whether there is any continuing business relationship.
explanation: Transaction records must be sufficient to reconstruct individual transactions to provide evidence for prosecution of criminal activity if necessary. The retention period is independent of whether the customer relationship continues. (PDF p.268)

section: Procedures for disclosure as suggested by the JFIU
front: To which body should disclosures of suspicious transactions under the DTRPO, OSCO or UNATMO be made, and on whom does the reporting obligation rest?
back: Disclosures should be made to the JFIU; the obligation rests with the individual who becomes suspicious of a person, transaction or property.
explanation: If suspicion remains after internal review, the report should be made without delay, by telephone if necessary. The firm, its directors and staff must not inform or warn the client when information is disclosed to the JFIU. (PDF p.271)

front: With what type of financial institution is a cross-border correspondent relationship prohibited?
back: A financial institution that has no physical presence in the place where it is authorised and is not a member of a regulated financial group subject to effective group-wide supervision.
explanation: This prohibition addresses the heightened ML/TF risk of dealing with institutions that lack real presence and group-wide oversight. By contrast, a relationship with a related foreign financial institution may even qualify for a streamlined due diligence approach relying on the group AML/CFT programme. (PDF p.271)

section: Electronic trading and alternative liquidity pools ("ALP")
front: Which part of the Code of Conduct sets out the specific conduct requirements for electronic trading (internet trading, DMA and algorithmic trading)?
back: Paragraph 18 and Schedule 7 of the Code of Conduct.
explanation: These provisions respond to the particular risks posed by internet trading, direct market access and algorithmic trading, and must be read together with the other conduct requirements of the Code of Conduct. ALPs are governed separately by Paragraph 19 and Schedule 8. (PDF p.274)

front: For how long must documentation of an electronic trading system's risk management controls, and audit logs/incident reports for material system delays or failures, be kept?
back: Risk management control documentation: not less than two years after the system ceased to be used. Audit logs and incident reports for material delays or failures: not less than two years.
explanation: Records of the design and development of the system (including testing, reviews, modifications, upgrades and rectifications) must also be kept. Note the two-year period here contrasts with the five-year AML retention periods for customer and transaction records. (PDF p.275)

front: How often must an algorithmic trading system and its trading algorithms be reviewed and tested?
back: Regularly, and no less than annually.
explanation: The review covers the system's ability to handle sizable trading volume and the algorithms' ability to execute orders without interfering with the operation of a fair and orderly market. Adequate testing is also required before deployment of any new development or modification, and due diligence should be performed on third-party-provided systems or algorithms. (PDF p.278)

section: Guidelines for Reducing and Mitigating Hacking Risks Associated with Internet Trading
front: What authentication method must a licensed or registered person implement for clients to log in to internet trading accounts?
back: Two-factor authentication.
explanation: The firm must also notify clients promptly (e.g. via email, SMS or push notification) after specified activities such as system login, password reset and trade execution, and implement an effective monitoring and surveillance mechanism with a strong encryption algorithm. (PDF p.278)

front: How frequently, and in what medium, should records and databases supporting internet trading be backed up?
back: At least daily, in an off-line medium; the firm must also ensure successful roll-back of major system changes.
explanation: This is one of the network infrastructure requirements under the SFC's hacking-risk guidelines, alongside a Demilitarised Zone with multi-tiered firewalls, need-to-have access, timely security patches, physical security for critical components, and effective contingency procedures. Cybersecurity awareness training must separately be provided to relevant staff at least yearly. (PDF p.279)

section: Conduct requirements of ALPs in Code of Conduct
front: Which part of the Code of Conduct applies to a licensed or registered person who operates an ALP or routes client orders to an ALP, and what is the defining feature of an ALP?
back: Paragraph 19 and Schedule 8 of the Code of Conduct; an ALP crosses/matches orders in exchange-listed or traded securities with no pre-trade transparency.
explanation: An ALP is an electronic system, in-house or third-party developed, operated by a licensed or registered person. ALPs are allowed to transact both Hong Kong listed and overseas listed securities, and the ALP operator must effectively manage and adequately supervise the design, development, deployment and operation of the ALP. (PDF p.279)

front: What insured amount does Schedule 2 of the Insurance Rules specify for dealing in securities, dealing in futures contracts or securities margin financing?
back: HK$15 million each, with a deductible amount not exceeding HK$3 million in each case. There is no specified amount for other regulated activities.
explanation: Schedule 2, Insurance Rules sets the cover per regulated activity; the HK$15 million insured amount and HK$3 million maximum deductible apply to each of the three named activities, and these exact figures are frequently tested. (PDF p.288)

front: Under which SFO section is maintaining insurance cover (or depositing security with the SFC) a necessary condition for the granting of a licence, and under which section were the Insurance Rules made?
back: Insurance cover or deposit of security is a licensing condition under s. 118, SFO; the SFC made the Securities and Futures (Insurance) Rules under s. 116(5), SFO.
explanation: Two distinct SFO provisions are involved: s. 118 imposes the condition for granting a licence, while s. 116(5) is the rule-making power for the Insurance Rules. Confusing the two section numbers is a common error. (PDF p.288)

front: Which licensed corporations are NOT subject to the Insurance Rules?
back: Those with a licensing condition that they may not hold client assets AND that are not exchange participants of either the SEHK or the Hong Kong Futures Exchange Limited.
explanation: Both limbs must be met for the exemption: a no-client-asset licensing condition and non-participation in both exchanges. The risks covered by the required insurance are losses of client assets, including those held by an associated entity, attributable to, for example, fraud or theft. (PDF p.288)

front: How many data protection principles must data users comply with under the PDPO, and where are they set out?
back: Six data protection principles, set out in Schedule 1 to the PDPO.
explanation: The six principles cover: (1) purpose and manner of collection, (2) accuracy and duration of retention, (3) use, (4) security, (5) information to be generally available, and (6) access to personal data. (PDF p.283)

front: Who enforces and promotes compliance with the PDPO?
back: The Privacy Commissioner for Personal Data, an independent public officer appointed to enforce and promote compliance with the PDPO.
explanation: If a data user refuses a data subject's request to correct incorrect data, the data subject can report the matter to the Privacy Commissioner for Personal Data — not to the SFC. (PDF p.283)

section: Access to and operation of ALPs
front: Who counts as a "qualified investor" permitted to use an ALP?
back: A professional investor who is (i) an Institutional Professional Investor, or (ii) a Corporate Professional Investor — except corporations acting solely as investment holding companies wholly owned by an Individual Professional Investor.
explanation: Only orders placed or originated by qualified investors may be routed to the ALP. Individual Professional Investors are not qualified investors, and the carve-out also excludes their wholly owned investment holding companies. (PDF p.280)

section: Access to and operation of ALPs
front: In an ALP, which orders have priority when a non-proprietary order and a proprietary order are being transacted at the same price?
back: Non-proprietary orders have priority over proprietary orders at the same price, irrespective of the time when the orders were placed.
explanation: A proprietary order is one for the account of the ALP operator or a group company trading as principal, any account in which they have an interest, or the account of their employees or agents. Time priority does not override this client-first rule. (PDF p.280)

front: What products are allowed to be crossed/matched in an ALP?
back: Hong Kong listed securities and overseas listed securities.
explanation: This is the knowledge check answer on permitted ALP products: both Hong Kong listed and overseas listed securities qualify, but futures contracts and unlisted securities are not mentioned. (PDF p.290)

front: Who developed the CRS and when, and which Hong Kong ordinance implements it?
back: The OECD developed the CRS in 2014; in Hong Kong it is implemented by the Inland Revenue (Amendment) (No.3) Ordinance 2016, which requires annual reporting to the Inland Revenue Department.
explanation: Licensed corporations and registered institutions must perform due diligence to identify financial accounts held by tax residents of reportable jurisdictions — those with which Hong Kong has an automatic exchange of financial account information (AEOI) agreement — and report annually to the Inland Revenue Department. (PDF p.288)

front: What are the OECD's core principles of corporate governance practices?
back: Fairness, transparency, accountability and responsibility.
explanation: The OECD issued this set of core principles; governance itself is the systems and processes by which companies are directed and controlled to fulfil business objectives, covering the relationships between management, board, shareholders and stakeholders. (PDF p.286)

front: In Topic 7 ("Participating in the Hong Kong exchanges"), which exchange is named in section 5 on dealing in futures contracts, and what other sub-headings does that section cover?
back: Hong Kong Futures Exchange Limited. Section 5 covers: Hong Kong Futures Exchange Limited; Trading; Clearing participants; Participant requirements; Ongoing requirements; Clearing and margin; and Settlement.
explanation: The Topic 7 table of contents lists "Hong Kong Futures Exchange Limited" as the first sub-heading of section 5, "Dealing in futures contracts", followed by trading, clearing participants, participant requirements, ongoing requirements, clearing and margin (p.292) and settlement (p.293). Do not confuse this with section 4, which deals with traded options on the SEHK. (PDF p.292)

front: What is the current fee for a trading right on the SEHK, and can trading rights be transferred?
back: Trading rights are only available from the SEHK for a fee currently set at HK$500,000, and they are non-transferable.
explanation: Anyone wishing to trade through the SEHK or the HKFE must hold trading rights and register as an exchange participant of the respective exchange. Those wishing to clear trades must also become clearing participants of the relevant clearing houses (HKSCC, SEOCH and/or HKCC). (PDF p.299)

front: Name the four clearing houses in Hong Kong and state what each clears.
back: HKSCC (wholly owned by HKEX) — cash business and securities settlement of the SEHK; SEOCH (wholly owned) — options business of the SEHK; HKCC (wholly owned) — futures and options business of the HKFE; OTC Clear (majority-owned) — OTC derivatives transactions.
explanation: The HKSCC operates CCASS for SEHK securities transactions, while DCASS is the common derivatives platform operated by the HKCC for HKFE futures and options and by the SEOCH for Traded Options. OTC Clear started operation in 2013 and is designated by the SFC as a central counterparty for certain OTC derivative transactions subject to the mandatory clearing obligation. (PDF p.298)

front: Which Mainland investors may participate in Southbound trading under the Stock Connects?
back: Only (a) institutional investors and (b) individual investors who satisfy the eligibility criteria — presently holding at least RMB500,000 in their securities and cash accounts.
explanation: All other Mainland investors are excluded from Southbound trading, and Mainland investors are prohibited from buying or selling eligible securities via Northbound trading under the Stock Connects. Mainland investors include individuals with Mainland ID documents, joint accounts with such a holder, and entities registered in the Mainland. (PDF p.301)

section: Northbound trading via the Shanghai Connect
front: What are the eligibility criteria for a constituent stock of the SSE A Share Index to qualify for Northbound trading?
back: (i) Daily average market capitalisation of at least RMB 5 billion in the last six months; (ii) daily average turnover of at least RMB 30 million in the last six months; (iii) not suspended from trading on any SSE Market on 50% or more of the trading days in the last six months; (iv) if it has differentiated voting rights, additional criteria apply.
explanation: Alternatively, an SSE-listed A share traded in RMB is eligible if it has corresponding H shares listed on the SEHK. An eligible A share that later fails the criteria or is placed under risk alert may be designated sell-only, meaning it cannot be bought. (PDF p.302)

front: What are the daily trading quotas under the Stock Connects, and how is the quota calculated?
back: Northbound daily quota: RMB52 billion for each of the Shanghai Connect and the Shenzhen Connect. Southbound daily quota: RMB42 billion for each. Calculated on a 'net buy' basis — sell trades increase available quota, and sells are always allowed regardless of the quota balance.
explanation: Buy trades are subject to there being available daily quota, while investors may always sell their cross-border securities. Trading of A shares and ETFs shares the same daily quota. (PDF p.304)

front: What are the key eligibility criteria for an SEHK-listed ETF to be tradable Southbound under the Stock Connects?
back: It must (a) be primarily regulated by the SFC; (b) have been listed for at least six months, with its benchmark index launched for at least one year; (c) be traded in HKD with daily average AUM in the last six months of no less than HKD 550 million; and (d) have a benchmark index meeting requirements on the total weighting of eligible SEHK-listed stocks and the benchmark methodology.
explanation: Compare the Northbound side: an SSE-listed ETF must be traded in RMB with daily average AUM in the last six months of no less than RMB 500 million, with the same six-month listing and one-year benchmark requirements. An ETF that later fails the criteria may be designated sell-only. (PDF p.304)

front: What are the settlement timetables for Southbound and Northbound executed trades under the Stock Connects?
back: Southbound trades settle on the usual T+2 basis, as for other SEHK trades. Northbound trades settle on the trade day for securities settlement, and on the trade day or a T+1 basis for money settlement.
explanation: For Northbound orders the HKSCC, acting through its Mainland incorporated subsidiary, is a participant of ChinaClear and undertakes the settlement obligations of the CCCPs. For Southbound orders, ChinaClear's Hong Kong subsidiary acts as a Special Participant of the HKSCC, but the HKSCC remains the central clearing party as for all SEHK trades. (PDF p.305)

section: Investor identification regime for Northbound trading
front: By when must a CCEP send the BCAN-to-client identification mapping information file to SEHK in order to input a Northbound order on T day?
back: On or before T-1 day, and the file must have passed the validation of SEHK. The CCEP must also provide the corresponding BCAN in every Northbound order inputted.
explanation: Each Northbound client is assigned a unique BCAN in the SEHK standard format, which must not be changed or re-used for other clients, mapped to the client's name and the issuing country, type and number of the identity document. Without a validated mapping file delivered by T-1, Northbound orders cannot be input on T day. (PDF p.306)

front: At what thresholds does the SSE/SZSE suspend and resume margin trading in an ETF under the Northbound trading links?
back: Suspend after both the balance of margin trading and the market value of the collaterals in the margin account reach 75% of the ETF's listed and tradable market capitalisation; resume when any one of the percentages drops below 70%.
explanation: These ETF thresholds (75%/70%) contrast with the thresholds for shares, where margin trading is suspended when the margin trading indicator reaches 25% and resumes when it drops below 20%. Margin trading is permitted only for CCEPs and exchange participants registered with SEHK to conduct Northbound trading through CCEPs for the account of their clients. (PDF p.308)

front: What are the maximum permitted periods for SBL agreements under the Northbound trading links?
back: For covered short selling: not longer than one calendar month. For meeting pre-trade checking requirements: not longer than one day, and roll-over is not allowed.
explanation: SBL is permitted only for these two purposes, only certain types of persons as determined by SSE or SZSE may act as stock lenders, and all SBL activities must be reported to the SEHK. Securities not eligible for both buy and sell orders may not be subject to SBL for the purpose of covered short selling. (PDF p.308)

front: What foreign ownership limits does the Chinese Mainland impose on listed companies?
back: A single foreign investor cannot own more than 10% of a listed company's issued shares, and total foreign investors' ownership cannot exceed 30% of total issued shares. ETFs listed on the SSE or SZSE are not subject to shareholding restrictions.
explanation: The HKEX publishes a notification on its website when the SSE/SZSE inform it that these limits are being approached, and foreign investors will be requested to sell shares if the 30% threshold is exceeded. A separate Mainland disclosure rule applies at the 5% holding level and may impact a person's ability to trade during specified periods. (PDF p.308)

section: Short selling via the Northbound trading links
front: What quantity limits apply to short selling a security via the Northbound trading links?
back: Not more than 1% of the shares of that short selling security held by all investors through the CCASS as at the commencement of that trading day, and not more than 5% cumulatively in any period of ten consecutive trading days.
explanation: In addition, a short selling order must not be input at a price lower than the most recent execution price, or the previous closing price if there have been no executed trades. The HKEX publishes each trading day the number of securities available for short selling, updated every 15 minutes during the trading day. (PDF p.309)

section: SFC Transaction Levy and AFRC Transaction Levy
front: What levies does the SEHK charge on each purchase and sale of securities, and at what rates?
back: SFC Transaction Levy of 0.0027% and AFRC Transaction Levy of 0.00015% (each rounded to the nearest cent), calculated as a percentage of each transaction and paid by both the buyer and the seller.
explanation: The Investor Compensation Levy of 0.002%, formerly charged by the SFC for the Investor Compensation Fund, has been suspended from 19 December 2005 onward. No levy is charged on Securities Market Maker transactions. (PDF p.314)

front: What are the amounts of the transfer deed stamp duty and the transfer fee, and who pays each?
back: Transfer deed stamp duty: HK$5 per new transfer deed, levied by the Government and payable by the registered holder of the share certificate(s), i.e. the seller. Transfer fee: HK$2.5 per share certificate for each new certificate issued, levied by the listed company's registrar on the registered holder, i.e. the buyer.
explanation: Both charges apply irrespective of the quantity of shares traded. By contrast, stamp duty on the transaction itself is 0.1% of the value (rounded up to the nearest dollar) payable by both buyer and seller, and the Trading Fee payable to the SEHK is 0.00565% per side. (PDF p.315)

front: What minimum collateral must an Exchange Participant engaged in SBL collect, and how often must borrowings be marked to market?
back: Collateral of at least 100% of the value of the securities loaned, or 105% if the borrowing is for short selling; all securities borrowings must be marked to market at least daily against the previous day's closing price.
explanation: These are the two ongoing SBL obligations of an Exchange Participant under the Rules of the Exchange. Note the 5-percentage-point uplift when the borrowing supports short selling, and that the daily mark-to-market uses the previous day's closing price. (PDF p.318)

front: What is the reportable short position threshold under the Short Position Reporting Rules, and what are the reporting deadlines?
back: For specified shares (other than CIS interests): a net short position value equal to or more than the LOWER of HK$30 million and 0.02% of the value of the total specified shares issued. For CIS interests: HK$30 million or more. Report to the SFC within 2 business days after the reporting day (normally the last trading day of each week); within 1 business day if the SFC has published a daily reporting requirement notice.
explanation: The dual threshold (lower of HK$30m / 0.02%) applies only to corporate shares; CIS interests use the flat HK$30m figure. The SFC may switch from weekly to daily reporting when circumstances threaten the financial stability of Hong Kong, cutting the deadline from two business days to one. (PDF p.319)

front: On what day are SEHK trades settled in CCASS, and what happens if a CCASS Clearing Participant has insufficient stock to settle?
back: Almost all SEHK trades are settled on T+2 by electronic debit and credit entries to stock accounts. If the participant lacks sufficient stock by the end of T+2, the HKSCC executes a compulsory buy-in on T+3; the short participant bears the cost of the shares purchased and is subject to a default fee.
explanation: Remember the sequence: settlement on T+2, compulsory buy-in on T+3. The defaulting participant pays both the cost of the bought-in shares and a default fee. (PDF p.321)

front: By what time must money settlement of the SEOCH's daily mark-to-market margin be confirmed, and through what system is it collected?
back: Money settlement conducted by settlement banks should be confirmed before 9:15am, so all open positions are fully margined before the markets open. Collections from SEOCH Clearing Participants are processed by the Direct Margin Debiting System, through which the SEOCH instructs its settlement banks to direct debit the participant's bank account.
explanation: Each day after the close of trading, the SEOCH marks marginable positions to market using the fixing price of each option series it determines; the resulting amount is the mark-to-market margin. The 9:15am confirmation deadline ensures full margining before market open. (PDF p.326)

front: Within what time must a SEOCH intra-day margin call be met, and what level of margin erosion automatically triggers it?
back: All intra-day margin calls must be met by SEOCH Clearing Participants within one hour after notification. Generally, a margin erosion of 50% in the margin interval of any one of the option classes automatically triggers the intra-day margin call.
explanation: Intra-day calls are made during periods of high market volatility on real-time open positions using prevailing market prices. They let the SEOCH restore margins immediately for contracts whose prices have changed significantly, instead of waiting for next-day settlement. (PDF p.327)

front: In disciplining Exchange Participants, what matters fall to the SFC and what matters fall to the SEHK?
back: The SFC, as front-line regulator of intermediaries, is responsible for all disciplinary matters relating to breaches of the SFO, the statutory rules, regulations and codes of conduct. The SEHK handles disciplinary matters other than those within the SFC's purview, specifically trading, clearing and settlement offences and breaches of the Listing Rules.
explanation: Under Chapter 7, Rules of the Exchange, an Exchange Participant is responsible for the acts of its responsible officers, directors, partners, authorised persons, representatives, officers and employees. SEHK disciplinary actions include warning letters, fines and suspension of exchange participantship. (PDF p.323)

section: Trading position limits and reporting requirements
front: What are the prescribed position limits for SEHK stock futures, Hang Seng Index futures, HSCEI futures and SEHK stock/ETF options under the Contracts Limits Rules?
back: SEHK stock futures: 25,000 contracts (all contract months combined). Hang Seng Index futures: 15,000 net long or short positions (all contract periods combined). Hang Seng China Enterprises Index futures: 25,000 net long or short positions (all contract periods combined). SEHK stock and ETF options: 250,000 contracts per option class in any one market direction for all expiries combined.
explanation: These are the four example limits the syllabus specifies under the Securities and Futures (Contracts Limits and Reportable Positions) Rules; holding or controlling positions in excess of them without authorisation by the SFC or the exchanges is prohibited, and violation is an offence subject to potential fine and imprisonment. (PDF p.333)

section: Trading position limits and reporting requirements
front: Within what time must a reportable position be notified under the Contracts Limits Rules, and to whom?
back: It must be notified in writing and lodged with the exchange company concerned (the HKFE or the SEHK as appropriate) within one business day following the day on which first holding/control occurs, and on each succeeding day in which the position continues.
explanation: Reporting is a separate obligation from the position limits themselves, and breaching either the holding or the reporting provisions is an offence subject to potential fine and imprisonment. The reporting levels include 1,000 contracts (any one contract month) for SEHK stock futures and 1,000 contracts per option class per expiry for SEHK stock and ETF options. (PDF p.333)

front: When must an HKFE Participant issue a margin call for a new position established by an established client without prior collateral?
back: T Session position: the call for the minimum margin must be issued within that business day. T+1 Session position: the call must be issued within the next business day. The client must be advised the margin is due as soon as practicable, and in no event later than the next business day of the relevant session; no new positions are allowed while margin calls are overdue.
explanation: These concessions apply only to established clients (those with a record of consistently meeting margin obligations, a sound financial position, and a representation that funds will be immediately transmitted). They do not apply to day trades for established clients with a history of transacting day trades exclusively, who must provide adequate collateral first. (PDF p.331)

front: What is the Northbound daily trading link quota for the Shanghai Connect and the Shenzhen Connect?
back: RMB52 billion for each of the Shanghai Connect and the Shenzhen Connect.
explanation: This is a hard monetary threshold tested in the knowledge check: each Connect link has its own Northbound daily quota of RMB52 billion. (PDF p.337)

front: Which Chinese Mainland investors may participate in Southbound trading under the Connect links?
back: Only institutional investors, and individual investors who hold RMB500,000 or more in their securities and cash accounts.
explanation: Not all Mainland persons can trade Southbound: the monetary eligibility threshold for individual investors is RMB500,000 held in securities and cash accounts, while institutional investors qualify without that test. (PDF p.337)

section: Persons involved in listings - Controlling shareholders
front: Under the Listing Rules, what voting threshold makes a person (or group acting together) a controlling shareholder of an issuer?
back: 30% or more of the voting shares (excluding votes attaching to treasury shares), or being in a position to control the composition of a majority of the board of directors.
explanation: The definition has two limbs: the 30% voting threshold (treasury share votes excluded) or board control. Identifying controlling shareholders matters for ownership continuity, INED independence, suitability for listing and continuing connected transactions; a sponsor must do due diligence beyond merely examining the shareholder register. (PDF p.346)

front: What are the minimum INED requirements for the board of a listed issuer, and what shareholding levels affect a proposed INED's independence?
back: At least 3 INEDs (at least one with appropriate professional qualifications or accounting/financial management expertise); INEDs must be at least one-third of the board. Holding more than 1% of issued shares (excluding treasury shares) is an independence factor; 5% or more means the person will normally not be considered independent.
explanation: MBLR 3.10 and GLR 5.05 set the three-INED minimum and qualification requirement; MBLR 3.10A and GLR 5.05A set the one-third proportion. The 1% and 5% shareholding thresholds are factors under MBLR 3.13, and the same factors also apply to the director's immediate family members. (PDF p.344)

section: Persons involved in listings - Compliance advisers
front: What is the minimum period for which a listed issuer must retain a compliance adviser after initial listing, and what is the minimum SFC licence a sponsor must hold?
back: Compliance adviser: at least until publication of the issuer's financial results for the first full financial year after listing (MBLR 3A.19; GLR 6A.19). Sponsor: at minimum a Type 6 licence (advising on corporate finance), plus additional SFC eligibility requirements.
explanation: The compliance adviser must be appointed at the start of the initial listing and the SEHK may direct an issuer to appoint one even after the minimum period (MBLR 3A.20; GLR 6A.20). The sponsor's Type 6 minimum is stated at section 1.16, with extra eligibility requirements for corporate finance advisers acting as sponsors. (PDF p.349)

front: What are the revenue threshold and initial market capitalisation requirements for specialist technology companies (STCs) listing as a Commercial Company versus a Pre-Commercial Company?
back: A Commercial Company has revenue of at least HK$250 million for its most recent audited financial year and needs an initial market capitalisation of HK$4 billion; a Pre-Commercial Company (revenue below HK$250 million) needs an initial market capitalisation of HK$8 billion.
explanation: The HK$250 million revenue threshold is what separates the two categories of STC, and the lower-revenue Pre-Commercial Companies must compensate with double the initial market capitalisation (HK$8 billion versus HK$4 billion). Pre-Commercial Companies also face additional disclosure requirements and restrictions on fundamental changes to their principal business. (PDF p.354)

section: The SEHK's power to cancel a listing
front: After how long a continuous trading suspension may the SEHK cancel an issuer's listing, and under which rule?
back: The SEHK may cancel a listing where the issuer has been suspended from trading for a continuous period of 18 months (MBLR 6.01A(1)).
explanation: The 18-month continuous suspension is one of three additional cancellation grounds, alongside insufficient securities in public hands (MBLR 13.32B) and the issuer or its business no longer being suitable for listing. It is separate from cancellation for insufficient operations or assets under MBLR 13.24. (PDF p.359)

front: What net asset value must an issuer of non-collateralised derivative warrants have under MBLR Chapter 15A if it is not regulated by the HKMA or SFC and is not a government or state?
back: A net asset value (NAV) of not less than HK$2 billion, together with the required credit rating.
explanation: Under MBLR Chapter 15A, an issuer of non-collateralised derivative warrants (warrants with no securities to support them) must have an NAV of at least HK$2 billion and the required credit rating, unless it is regulated by the HKMA or the SFC or is a government or state. The issuer must also be incorporated and must not be a private company, and any guarantor must meet the same requirements. (PDF p.363)

front: At what level of voting rights does the "trigger" for a mandatory general offer arise under Rule 26 of the Takeovers Code?
back: When any person, or persons acting in concert, acquire voting rights which increase their holding to 30% or more of the voting rights of the company.
explanation: The Takeovers Code focuses on control of voting rights: it is enough that a person controls the voting rights, without owning the shares or being the registered shareholder. The Executive may grant a dispensation in certain circumstances, but this should not be assumed. (PDF p.367)

front: State the "creeper" rule that requires a mandatory offer under the Takeovers Code.
back: A person (or concert parties) holding not less than 30% but not more than 50% of the voting rights must make a mandatory offer if they increase their holding by more than 2% from their lowest percentage holding in the 12-month period ending on (and including) the date of the acquisition.
explanation: The creeper sits alongside the trigger (reaching 30% or more) as the two categories of circumstances requiring a Rule 26 mandatory offer. Any mandatory offer must include a cash component at not less than the highest price paid by the offeror or concert parties during, or within six months before, the offer period. (PDF p.368)

front: Intermediaries licensed or registered for which regulated activities benefit from the SFC's specific exemption from the advertisement authorisation requirement, and what products are carved out of that exemption?
back: Intermediaries licensed or registered for Type 1, Type 4 or Type 6 regulated activity, for Advertisements in respect of securities - except unlisted securities that are structured products, and unauthorised CISs.
explanation: Section 103(1), SFO makes it an offence to issue an Advertisement containing an invitation to the public regarding securities, structured products or CISs unless it is authorised by the SFC or exempt. This Type 1/4/6 carve-out is one such exemption; Advertisements for an authorised CIS must still comply with the Advertising Guidelines Applicable to Collective Investment Schemes Authorized under the Product Codes. (PDF p.375)

front: What minimum financial resources must a management company of an SFC-authorised CIS have under the UT Code?
back: Minimum paid-up share capital and non-distributable capital reserves of HK$10 million, or its foreign currency equivalent. It must also have no material lending and positive net assets at all times.
explanation: The UT Code requires a management company to have adequate financial resources, defined specifically as HK$10 million minimum paid-up share capital and non-distributable capital reserves (or foreign currency equivalent). The same HK$10 million figure applies to trustees/custodians not licensed or registered for Type 13 regulated activity. (PDF p.383)

front: What experience must the key personnel of a CIS management company have, and how many key personnel are generally required?
back: Key personnel must have at least five years' experience managing reputable public funds with the same or similar type of investments as those proposed to be managed. In general, at least two key personnel must be designated for each of the management company and any investment delegate.
explanation: The UT Code expects key personnel with at least five years' public funds experience and a demonstrable investment track record, dedicating sufficient time to managing the CIS. Generally at least two key personnel are designated per management company and investment delegate, with up-to-date records made available to the SFC on request. (PDF p.384)

front: What minimum net asset value (NAV) must an issuer of an authorised unlisted SIP have under Appendix A of the SIP Code?
back: A minimum NAV of HK$2 billion, and it must be a bank regulated by the HKMA, a corporation licensed by the SFC, or an overseas banking entity subject to regulatory oversight acceptable to the SFC (or, failing those, hold a top-three investment grade credit rating from an acceptable international rating agency).
explanation: Appendix A of the SIP Code sets the issuer eligibility threshold at a minimum NAV of HK$2 billion combined with one of three regulated statuses; a top-three investment grade rating from at least one acceptable rating agency is the fallback. A guarantor of an unlisted SIP must meet the same eligibility requirements as if it were the issuer. (PDF p.387)

front: For which regulated activity must a product arranger of an authorised unlisted SIP be licensed or registered?
back: Type 1 (dealing in securities) regulated activity. At the time of issue of the authorised unlisted SIP, it must also not be subject to any disciplinary proceedings in respect of its licence or registration, or any action by an exchange, regulated market or self-regulatory organisation for breach of applicable rules that may materially affect its financial condition, status or ability to perform its licensed or regulated activity.
explanation: Eligibility as a product arranger under the SIP Code rests on a Type 1 licence or registration plus a clean disciplinary position at the time of issue. An issuer may appoint more than one product arranger, but one must be designated as the primary channel of communication with the SFC. (PDF p.388)

section: Dual licensing regime for virtual asset trading platforms
front: Under the dual licensing regime, what licences does a centralised VA trading platform (VATP) need depending on the VAs it trades?
back: Trades VAs that are securities under the SFO: licensed or registered under the SFO (typically Type 1 and Type 7 regulated activity). Trades VAs that are not securities but meet the AMLO definition of VAs: licensed by the SFC under the AMLO. Engages in both: licensed under both the SFO and the AMLO.
explanation: The regime applies to centralised platforms trading VAs using an automated trading engine with custody services as an ancillary service. The licence required follows the nature of the VAs traded, and platforms straddling both categories need both an SFO and an AMLO licence. (PDF p.396)

front: What notification requirement is triggered when a licensed corporation or registered institution starts providing VA dealing/advisory services or VA asset management services?
back: The SFC regards providing dealing and advisory services in VA-related products and VA, as well as VA asset management services, as a significant change of activities, which triggers the notification requirement under the Securities and Futures (Licensing and Registration) (Information) Rules.
explanation: Beyond licensing, intermediaries moving into VA-related services must notify the SFC because this is treated as a significant change of activities under the Securities and Futures (Licensing and Registration) (Information) Rules. In addition, VA dealing services may only be provided by partnering with SFC-licensed VATPs and only by intermediaries licensed or registered for Type 1 regulated activity. (PDF p.397)

front: At what percentage of a company's total voting rights does the initial mandatory offer trigger arise, and what must the acquirer(s) then do?
back: The trigger arises when any person acquires, or two or more persons acting in concert acquire, voting rights bringing their holding to 30% or more of the total voting rights. The holders must then make an offer for each class of shares concerned.
explanation: 30% is the key initial threshold for a mandatory offer; it applies to a single acquirer or to persons acting in concert. A separate creeper rule catches those already holding 30-50% who increase their stake by more than 2% from their lowest holding in the preceding 12 months. (PDF p.400)

section: Orders made by the Market Misconduct Tribunal
front: What is the maximum duration of an MMT disqualification order and of a "cold shoulder order"?
back: Up to five years in each case: disqualification from holding office as a director, liquidator or receiver or from taking part in the management of a corporation for up to five years; and prohibition on investing or trading in Hong Kong markets for up to five years (the "cold shoulder order").
explanation: These are two of the orders the MMT may make under ss. 257 and 259, SFO against persons found to have committed market misconduct. Other available orders include payment of profit made or loss avoided to the Government plus compound interest, payment of Government and SFC costs, prohibition of the specified misconduct, and disciplinary referrals. (PDF p.412)

section: Criminal sanctions imposed by the courts
front: What are the maximum criminal sanctions for market misconduct under s. 303, SFO (a) on indictment and (b) on summary conviction?
back: (a) Ten years' imprisonment and HK$10 million on conviction on indictment; (b) three years' imprisonment and HK$1 million on summary conviction.
explanation: These are the criminal sanctions prescribed by the SFO under s. 303. In addition to these penalties, a court may impose sanctions such as the orders available to the MMT, e.g. disqualification and cold shoulder orders. (PDF p.413)

front: For insider dealing, what shareholding makes a person a "substantial shareholder" connected with a corporation?
back: Holding at least 5% of the total number of shares comprised in the relevant share capital of the corporation or its related corporation.
explanation: Persons connected with a corporation include directors, employees and substantial shareholders of the corporation and its related corporation, as well as persons with professional or business relationships giving expected access to inside information, and connected persons of another corporation where the inside information relates to transactions between the two corporations. (PDF p.407)

section: Market misconduct under the Securities and Futures Ordinance
front: What standard of proof applies (a) before the MMT and (b) in a criminal prosecution for market misconduct, and can both proceedings be brought against the same person for the same act?
back: (a) MMT: the civil standard, "on the balance of probabilities"; (b) criminal court: "beyond reasonable doubt". MMT proceedings and criminal prosecution cannot both be instituted against the same person for the same act of market misconduct.
explanation: The dual regime in Parts XIII and XIV, SFO lets the SFC choose the civil route (MMT, with the consent of the Secretary for Justice) or refer the facts to the Secretary for Justice for criminal prosecution, according to the nature of the acts and the strength of the available evidence. The SFC may also prosecute minor cases summarily in a magistrate's court under s. 388, SFO. (PDF p.405)

front: Within what time limits may a person rescind an agreement entered into as a result of an unlawful unsolicited call?
back: Written notice within 28 days after the day of entering into the agreement, or within 7 days after the day of finding out about the contravention — whichever is the EARLIER.
explanation: This rescission right protects investors who are pressured into agreements by cold calls prohibited under s. 174, SFO. Both limbs run from specific days and the earlier deadline applies, so the right can expire quickly once the contravention is discovered. (PDF p.415)

front: List the persons on whom an intermediary MAY make an unsolicited call (i.e. who are exempt from the s. 174, SFO cold-calling prohibition).
back: (a) existing clients; (b) licensed persons; (c) registered institutions; (d) professional investors; (e) solicitors or certified public accountants acting in their professional capacity; and (f) money lenders.
explanation: These categories are considered able to protect themselves, so the cold-calling prohibition does not apply to calls made to them. The Knowledge check confirms the same list for an investment manager carrying out cold calling. (PDF p.415)

front: What penalty did the SFC impose on the licensed corporation (LC-1) that breached Code of Conduct obligations relating to its electronic and algorithmic trading systems and alternative liquidity pool?
back: LC-1 was reprimanded and fined HK$17.3 million.
explanation: LC-1 (licensed for Types 1, 4 and 7) failed to control erroneous algorithmic orders, failed to give non-proprietary orders execution priority over proprietary orders in its ALP, and breached documentary requirements. Senior management cooperation, an independent review, and the board's undertaking to rectify failures within a fixed period helped LC-1 avoid a substantially higher fine. (PDF p.423)

front: What do the abbreviations MMT and MLRO stand for?
back: MMT = Market Misconduct Tribunal; MLRO = Money laundering reporting officer.
explanation: Both appear in the abbreviations list: MMT is the Market Misconduct Tribunal and MLRO is the money laundering reporting officer. Keep them distinct from SFAT, the Securities and Futures Appeals Tribunal, which is a different body. (PDF p.437)

front: Which clearing entities are abbreviated HKCC and HKSCC?
back: HKCC = HKFE Clearing Corporation Limited; HKSCC = Hong Kong Securities Clearing Company Limited.
explanation: The abbreviations list assigns HKCC to the futures-side clearer (HKFE Clearing Corporation Limited) and HKSCC to Hong Kong Securities Clearing Company Limited. A third clearing house, SEOCH, is The SEHK Options Clearing House Limited. (PDF p.436)

front: In the financial resources context, what does the abbreviation RLC stand for?
back: RLC = Required liquid capital.
explanation: The abbreviations list defines RLC as required liquid capital. It sits alongside the FRR — the Securities and Futures (Financial Resources) Rules — which is the related rules abbreviation, so the two should not be mixed up. (PDF p.438)

front: What does the abbreviation WMD(CPS)O stand for in the Paper 1 manual?
back: Weapons of Mass Destruction (Control of Provision of Services) Ordinance
explanation: The abbreviations list on the same page also includes UNSO (United Nations Sanctions Ordinance), so take care not to confuse the two sanctions-related ordinances. WMD(CPS)O specifically concerns the control of provision of services relating to weapons of mass destruction. (PDF p.439)
